question_text: A red wine DOC is described whose blends are built around Corvina as the signature grape. A separate DOC, Orta Nova in Apulia, permits blending with Uva di Troia, Lambrusco Maestri and Trebbiano. Which pairing correctly matches each classification to its principal grape or region?
options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': 'Both DOCs are anchored by Corvina, with Montepulciano as an optional blending grape'}, {'id': 'B', 'text': 'Both DOCs are anchored by Montepulciano, with Corvina permitted only in Orta Nova'}, {'id': 'C', 'text': 'Corvina-dominated DOC → Valpolicella; Orta Nova DOC blend partner → Montepulciano'}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'Corvina-dominated DOC → Montepulciano; Orta Nova DOC blend partner → Valpolicella'}]
source_facts:
[1] == Grapes and wine styles ==

Most of the wines produced under the Valpolicella DOC are red and usually contain a sizable amount of the area's most distinguished grape, Corvina.
---
[2] Can be blended with Cabernet Sauvignon
Orta Nova DOC (Apulia) – can be blended with Uva di Troia, Lambrusco Maestri and Trebbiano.

question_text: A Washington winemaker is considering whether to bottle a Malbec on its own instead of using it only to deepen the color and fruit profile of blends, as was more common historically in Bordeaux. In trial lots from a high-desert site with glacially derived soils, the wine shows ripe raspberry and dark-fruit character with medium acidity and tannin. Based on these observations, which vineyard source best supports releasing the varietal wine?
options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': 'A Malbec block in the Lake Chelan AVA'}, {'id': 'B', 'text': 'Any Washington Malbec site, since glacial soils there generally give the same profile'}, {'id': 'C', 'text': 'A Washington site outside the high-desert exception, where Malbec would be expected to follow the broader state pattern'}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'A Bordeaux parcel where Malbec is intended mainly to add color and fruit to blends'}]
source_facts:
[1] Malbec grapes grown in the glacial soils found within the Lake Chelan AVA produce wines possessing ripe raspberry - black fruit aromas and flavors with moderate acidity and tannins.
---
[2] An exception to this in Washington State is Malbec grown in the high desert Lake Chelan AVA terroir.
---
[3] Though the grape was historically a major planting in Bordeaux, providing color and fruit to its blends.

question_text: A winemaker in Cafayate, Argentina is looking to produce a high-quality white wine from Torrontés Riojano grapes. The region's dry climate during the growing season helps maintain the health of the grapes by limiting fungal diseases and rot. However, the rural location of the vineyards means the harvested grapes must be transported for several hours to reach the winemaking facilities in more urban areas. What should the winemaker do to ensure the grapes arrive in the best possible condition?
options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': 'Spray the grapes with water to prevent dehydration during the long journey'}, {'id': 'B', 'text': 'Harvest the grapes during the coolest part of the day to minimize oxidation and bacterial growth during transport'}, {'id': 'C', 'text': 'Allow the grapes to warm up before transport to promote the development of beneficial compounds'}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'Crush the grapes at the vineyard and transport the juice instead to reduce the risk of spoilage'}]
source_facts:
[1] Argentine wine particularly its sub-region of Cafayate, have been gaining the most worldwide attention the quality of its full bodied whites made from Torrontés Riojano as well as its fruity.
---
[2] Most of the growing season is dry with the lack of humidity limiting the risk and hazard from various grape diseases and fungal rot.
---
[3] After harvest, grapes often have to be transported long distances, taking several hours, from the rural vineyards to winemaking facilities located in more urban areas.

question_text: A Sardinian producer is finalizing the blend for a wine to be labeled under the island's main DOC for this grape, which local tradition calls Cannonau. The winemaker has three red lots from the 2023 harvest: a late-picked Cannonau lot brought in roughly two weeks after the estate's Cabernet Sauvignon parcel, a Carignan lot, and a Syrah lot. The cellar team proposes a final blend of 82% Cannonau, 12% Carignan, and 6% Syrah, arguing this balances fruit concentration with structure. Why must the winemaker reject this proposed blend if the wine is to be sold under this DOC?
options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': 'The DOC prohibits the inclusion of any Syrah in the blend regardless of percentage.'}, {'id': 'B', 'text': 'Because Cannonau typically ripens weeks before Cabernet Sauvignon, the late-picked lot likely suffered hangtime issues and cannot be used in a DOC wine.'}, {'id': 'C', 'text': 'The DOC requires that the designated local grape make up at least 90% of the blend, so Cannonau at 82% falls short of the legal minimum.'}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'DOC rules require the Cannonau to be harvested before the Cabernet Sauvignon parcel to qualify for appellation labeling.'}]
source_facts:
[1] Grenache history in the Rhône can be traced to the influence of Burgundian wine merchants in the 17-18th centuries who sought a blending variety to add body and alcohol content.
---
[2] wine Cannonau di Sardegna is by law at least 90% local Grenache (Cannonau).
---
[3] Grenache is often one of the last grapes to be harvested, often ripening weeks after Cabernet Sauvignon.
